input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation is grounded in Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) practices.  Core modeling is performed in SysML v1.7, leveraging Information Block Diagrams (IBDs) for component‑connector architecture and possibly state‑machine models for dynamic behavior.  Architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc are used to capture structural composition and inter‑connector relationships; MontiArc Automaton extensions provide finite‑state machine semantics for behavioral aspects.  Simulation of system models is conducted with the MathWorks Simulink engine, enabling continuous‑time dynamics and integration with MATLAB toolchains.  Derived code can be generated or hand‑coded in compiled languages such as C++ and Java, supporting real‑time execution or integration with embedded controllers.  These tools collectively form the technical enablers for creating, simulating, and operationalizing the Digital Twin within a heterogeneous modeling environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are articulated through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML v1.7 for functional and logical architecture, and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Behavioral representations are captured via state‑machine diagrams in SysML and MontiArc Automaton. Dynamic behavior is modeled and verified with MathWorks Simulink, with simulation data exported to code generators for C++ or Java. Models are organized across multiple abstraction levels—requirements, functional, logical, product/technical—providing abstractions and aggregations that link geometric, behavioral, and analytical aspects within the Digital Twin constellation.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle follows a Model‑Based Systems‑Engineering (MBSE) disciplined pathway comprising four abstraction layers: (1) requirements abstraction, where stakeholder needs are captured in system–level requirement documents; (2) functional architecture, described with SysML use‑case and activity diagrams and refined in component‑connector diagrams such as ADL snippets from ACME or SysML IBDs; (3) logical architecture, developed with architectural description languages (ADLs) like MontiArc or AADL automata that capture behavioral aspects; and (4) product/technical layers, where concrete implementation models are specified using code generators or Simulink block diagrams. Transition management between layers is governed by RFLP (Requirements‑Functions‑Logics‑Physics) methodology, ensuring traceability of functional intent through to physical entities. Lifecycle governance is performed with MBSE tools that support model versioning, change‑impact analysis, and artifact linking across the four layers.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning engineering process is based on established MBSE methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, which prescribe structured steps for requirement capture, function decomposition, logical and physical modeling, and system validation. Quality assurance relies on simulation‑driven validation using MathWorks Simulink models or MontiArc automata, and unit‑testing of generated C++/Java artifacts. Version control and evolution management are implemented through standard VCS (e.g., Git) applied to MBSE artifacts and generated source code, with branching strategies reflecting Digital Twin versioning and rollback capability. Continuous integration pipelines automatically build and test simulation models, compile generated code, and perform model‑to‑artifact consistency checks, thereby managing incremental DT evolution with trace links to RFLP-derived artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standard compliance is achieved by adhering to open modeling standards: SysML v1.7 for functional and structural modeling, AADL for architecture description with real‑time properties, MontiArc for component‑connector behavioral modeling, and ACME for extensible architectural notation. These ADLs ensure interoperability with industry toolchains such as Enterprise Architect or Cameo, and provide mapping mechanisms to IEC 61131‑3 code templates for further implementation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
